artifacts.content:
Hey Becca,

Sitting in the break room for a minute and just needed to get this out of my head. Since that last Humira shot on 12/24, things have gone downhill faster than I expected. The week of 1/6 was rough enough that I missed two full shifts and had to leave early another day because my hands and knees just wouldn’t cooperate. The plaques on my fingers and the back of my neck were so obvious that a couple guys on line 3 asked if I’d burned myself.

Elena finally pulled me into her office on Thursday 1/9 here at the Round Rock warehouse. She tried to be kind, but basically said if I can’t keep up with lifting 50+ lb boxes and my attendance doesn’t get back to normal, she may have to start documenting performance issues. She even mentioned it could affect the 2025 merit raise and any talk of a promotion track. I get where she’s coming from, but it scared the hell out of me.

I keep thinking how crazy it is that Lone Star cut off the Humira right when it was actually working. I went from walking the floor and coaching Caleb’s team to barely making it through a shift and crashing on the couch. I’m trying the prednisone Keller gave me, but you know that’s not a long-term answer.

Tonight I’ll still do taco night with the kids, but I may need you to handle most of the cleanup. My fingers are already stiff from scanning pallets all morning. We’ll figure this out, I just don’t want the kids to see me slipping.

Love you,
Michael

metadata.subject: Work is starting to feel shaky

artifacts.content:
Dan,

Long one, sorry in advance. Things have been piling up since they stopped the Humira after that 12/24 dose. On 1/8 I was supposed to take Sophia to her first band concert at Cedar Ridge Middle School. She’s been practicing that clarinet solo in the living room for weeks. That afternoon my joints locked up so bad I didn’t feel safe driving from Willow Bend over there, and sitting through a whole concert sounded impossible. Rebecca ended up taking both Sophia and Caleb while I stayed home on the couch, trying not to scratch my arms bloody.

Bec told them my psoriasis and arthritis were “acting up again,” and when they got home we ended up in this long, heavy conversation in the kitchen about whether I’m going to end up disabled like Dad was in his 50s. Sophia flat-out asked if I’d still be able to come to her high school graduation. Caleb wanted to know if I’d still coach his baseball. I didn’t know how to answer without lying.

On top of that, I saw Dr. Keller at Seton Williamson on 1/3 because I wasn’t sleeping from the pain and itching. He said my plaques are back on about 15% of my body and my PHQ-9 jumped from 3 to 11 over the last few months. He started a prednisone taper and hydroxyzine 25 mg at night so I can at least knock out for a few hours.

I know you’ve got your own stuff in Dallas, but I just needed to tell someone who remembers what it was like with Dad. Call when you can.

-Mike

Hi Mom,

Just wanted to update you after our talk on Sunday, since I know you worry when I don’t spell everything out.

After Lone Star denied the Humira renewal on 12/20, things started flaring again. I booked an urgent visit with my primary doctor, Dr. Brian Keller, at the Seton Williamson Clinic here in Round Rock on 1/3. He wrote down that the psoriasis is back on roughly 15% of my body surface area – elbows, knees, scalp, and now my hands again – and that my sleep is shot because of the pain and itching. He started me on a short prednisone taper and added hydroxyzine 25 mg at bedtime so my brain will actually shut off for a bit.

He also did that depression questionnaire they always do. Back in the fall my PHQ-9 score was a 3, basically nothing. On 1/3 it was up to 11, which he said counts as “moderate.” I told him a lot of that is just the stress of the Humira being cut off and worrying about work and the kids seeing me like this.

Dr. Chang is still pushing the insurance to listen. She’s reminding them I’ve already had liver enzyme bumps on methotrexate and that with my nonalcoholic fatty liver disease, plus everything you’ve been going through with the cirrhosis workup at St. David’s Georgetown back in October, it doesn’t make sense to force more liver-toxic meds.

Don’t panic; I’m still going in to the warehouse most days, just moving a little slower. I’ll plan to come up next Sunday to take you to H‑E‑B unless the joints completely revolt.

Love,
Michael

Personal journal – 2025-01-10

Rough couple of weeks. Timeline so I don’t lose track:

12/20 – Lone Star denies Humira renewal and the exception to step therapy. They still want me to retry systemic drugs that already caused liver issues.

12/24 – Last approved Humira dose. I remember doing the shot after dinner, kids watching a Christmas movie.

Week of 1/6 – Flare hits hard. Missed two full shifts at Central Texas Distribution and left early once because joints wouldn’t let me keep lifting or even stand at the scanner. On 1/9 Elena Rodriguez pulled me into her office at the Round Rock facility. She said if I can’t consistently handle 50+ lb boxes and my attendance doesn’t improve, she may have to document it, which could hurt the 2025 merit raise and any promotion.

1/3 – Urgent visit with Dr. Keller at Seton Williamson. He measured about 15% BSA involvement. PHQ-9 up from 3 to 11 over the last three months – “moderate depression.” Started prednisone short course and hydroxyzine 25 mg at night for sleep/anxiety.

1/8 – Missed Sophia’s first band concert at Cedar Ridge Middle School because I couldn’t sit or drive comfortably from Willow Bend. Rebecca had to tell Sophia and Caleb it was my psoriasis/arthritis again. Ended up talking with them about whether I’ll end up disabled like Dad in his 50s. Hardest conversation I’ve had as a dad.

Need to remember: adalimumab gave me my life back for 18+ months. Without it, everything – work, parenting, even walking the neighborhood with Rebecca – feels shaky.

Subject: Liver stuff & meds

Hey Dan,

Following up on what we texted about. I saw Dr. Chang at Austin Dermatology Associates back on 11/15 and then again when her office sent more paperwork on 12/5. In both notes she spelled out that I’ve got nonalcoholic fatty liver disease with those intermittent ALT elevations and that when I was on methotrexate my liver enzymes spiked enough that my PCP told me to stop. She also mentioned that Mom was evaluated for suspected cirrhosis at St. David’s Georgetown Hospital in October 2024.

Her point to Lone Star is that, with my NAFLD and the prior ALT bumps on methotrexate, plus our family history with Mom’s liver, it’s really not safe to go back on methotrexate or try acitretin like they’re pushing. She explicitly recommended avoiding both in her 11/15 and 12/5 notes. Despite that, the plan still denied the exception to step therapy on 12/20 and cut off Humira after my 12/24 dose.

Meanwhile, the fallout is hitting everything else. I’ve already missed two shifts the week of 1/6 and left early another day. Elena at the Round Rock warehouse told me on 1/9 that if I can’t keep lifting 50+ lb boxes, she might have to start writing me up, which could kill any shot at a 2025 raise or promotion.

I’m trying to keep things normal for Sophia and Caleb – still helping with homework when my fingers aren’t too stiff and tossing the football when I can. But I’m not going to pretend I’m not scared about where this goes if they keep insisting on those liver-toxic meds.

Call when you get a breather.

Mike

Event: Follow-up with Dr. Lisa Chang – medication options

Date: Friday, 2025-02-14
Time: 3:15–4:00 p.m.
Location: Austin Dermatology Associates – 2nd floor clinic, Austin, TX
Attendees: Me; Dr. Chang

Notes to self:

– Bring list of all flares since last visit on 2024-11-15, especially the week of 1/6 when I missed two shifts and left early once at Central Texas Distribution because of joint pain and plaques on my hands/neck.
– Mention Elena Rodriguez’s talk with me on 1/9 at the Round Rock warehouse about attendance and lifting 50+ lb boxes, and how she said it could affect my 2025 merit raise/promotion if things don’t improve.
– Update her on 1/3 Seton Williamson visit with Dr. Keller: psoriasis back on ~15% BSA, PHQ-9 up from 3 to 11, started short prednisone taper and hydroxyzine 25 mg at bedtime for sleep/anxiety.
– Ask her to go over again why methotrexate and acitretin are bad ideas with my NAFLD, the previous ALT elevations, and Mom’s suspected cirrhosis workup at St. David’s Georgetown in October 2024 – I want to be able to explain it clearly to Rebecca and Mom.
– Check if we need new labs (CBC, CMP, TB) before restarting any biologic.
– Try to leave warehouse by 2:15 p.m. to beat some of the I‑35 traffic from Round Rock.

Reminder: text Rebecca when I leave work so she knows whether she’s on solo kid pickup duty from Cedar Ridge and the elementary school.

Personal note – late night worries (2025-01-13)

Couldn’t sleep again even after taking the hydroxyzine 25 mg Keller gave me. Joints ache, skin itches, and my brain won’t shut up.

Work: After missing those two shifts the week of 1/6 and ducking out early once, I’m terrified Elena’s going to have to start writing me up. Her talk with me on 1/9 at the Round Rock warehouse about attendance, lifting 50+ lb boxes, and the 2025 merit raise/promotion has been replaying in my head nonstop. I’ve worked too hard these last seven years at Central Texas Distribution to see it slip because an insurance company pulled the plug on Humira after 12/24.

Home: Still feel awful about bailing on Sophia’s first Cedar Ridge Middle School band concert on 1/8 because I couldn’t drive or sit through it. The look on her face when Rebecca told her I was staying home because my psoriasis and arthritis were “acting up again” gutted me. That conversation afterward about whether I’ll end up disabled like Dad in his 50s is one I never wanted to have with my kids.

Health: 1/3 visit with Dr. Keller keeps echoing too – 15% of my body covered again, PHQ-9 up from 3 to 11. I always thought of myself as pretty even-keeled, but this has knocked me sideways.

Goal for tomorrow: get through the full shift, walk the floor at least twice, and still have enough left to toss the football with Caleb for 15 minutes after dinner.

Notes to self:
